findings.text: Major | content — deck-systemic — first content slide labels a topic ("Solution Overview") instead of stating the deck's governing thesis — Pyramid Principle (Minto 1987) — Insert a thesis slide stating the single claim the deck argues for
findings.rationale: Pyramid Principle requires the top of the pyramid to state the deck's governing thesis as a single claim; the first content slide labels a topic instead, forcing the audience to derive the message themselves.
findings.fix: Insert a thesis slide stating: 'Our hybrid solution cuts onboarding from 6 weeks to 4 days for SMB customers.'

findings.text: Critical | content — deck-systemic — deck has no resolution slide; closes on "Strategic Direction" without naming the answer or the ask — Narrative arc (Duarte, Resonate 2010) — Add a resolution slide that names the recommended action and the ask
findings.rationale: Persuasive decks follow setup → tension → resolution; this deck stops at the problem framing without delivering the answer or the ask, leaving the audience without a clear next step.
findings.fix: Add a final resolution slide naming the specific recommendation and the explicit ask (e.g., 'Approve $2M FY26 budget for the hybrid rollout')

findings.text: Major | content — slide 2 body — claim "innovative and disruptive" carries no on-slide evidence (no number, source, or example) — Claim-evidence balance (Heath, Made to Stick 2007) — Replace adjectives with a quantified outcome and a source citation
findings.rationale: Hyperbolic adjectives without supporting data, customer story, or third-party citation give the audience no anchor to evaluate the claim.
findings.fix: Replace with a quantified outcome and source line, e.g., "Reduced onboarding 86% (Acme pilot, Q2 2026)"

findings.text: Minor | content — slide 4 body — bullet relies on 7 unintroduced acronyms (SaaS, B2B, SMB, GTM, PLG, ARR, ACV, ICP, MQLs, SQLs, BCG); a stranger reading the deck without a presenter cannot decode it — Standalone readability (Reynolds, Presentation Zen 2008; audience-fit per Knaflic 2015) — Spell out acronyms on first use; cap at ≤5 distinct per slide
findings.rationale: Standalone readability requires a slide to make sense without presenter narration; an unfiltered acronym block forces the reader into glossary lookups and breaks comprehension.
findings.standard: Standalone readability (Reynolds, Presentation Zen 2008; audience-fit per Knaflic 2015)
findings.fix: Spell out acronyms on first use; move definitions to speaker notes; cap at ≤5 distinct per slide
